It's a binary format that provides a high-level abstraction over GPU-specific instructions.
what_you_will_learn: Shader modules — writing SPIR-V code as separate modules for reusability and modularity
SPIR-V binary format — understanding the structure of SPIR-V files, including header, constants, and executable code
Vulkan shader objects — creating and managing SPIR-V shaders in Vulkan applications using VkShaderModule
SPIR-V validation and compilation — ensuring SPIR-V modules are valid and can be compiled into machine code by the GPU
SPIR-V optimization techniques — applying compiler optimizations to improve performance, such as dead code elimination and constant folding
SPIR-V extensions — understanding how to use Vulkan's extension mechanism to add custom functionality to SPIR-V shaders

knowledge.what_it_is: The Rendering Equation is a mathematical formula used to calculate the color of a pixel on a screen, taking into account the lighting, materials, and geometry of a scene. It's a fundamental concept in computer graphics and game development.
what_you_will_learn: Radiance equation — the core formula for calculating pixel colors, combining diffuse and specular components
BRDF (Blinn-Phong) — a simplified model for simulating material reflectance, used in many games and engines
GGX (Smith-GGX) — a more accurate BRDF model for simulating complex materials like metals and glass
Cook-Torrance — an advanced BRDF model for simulating detailed material properties like roughness and normal distribution
Ambient Occlusion (AO) — a technique for adding depth cues to scenes, used in many game engines
Screen Space Ambient Occlusion (SSAO) — a variant of AO that uses screen space techniques for improved performance
Light Transport — understanding how light interacts with scene geometry and materials, including diffuse, specular, and ambient components

knowledge.what_it_is: Visibility and occlusion are essential concepts in game development, referring to how objects or geometry are hidden from view due to other objects or surfaces. This affects rendering, collision detection, and overall gameplay experience.
what_you_will_learn: Z-buffering — a technique used for depth buffering, storing the minimum depth value encountered so far at each pixel
Stencil buffer — a separate buffer that stores stencil values, used to mask or clip objects based on their proximity to other surfaces
Occlusion culling — techniques and algorithms for reducing the number of objects rendered by removing those that are not visible to the camera
Frustum culling — a method of occlusion culling where objects outside the viewing frustum (camera's field of view) are removed from rendering
Portal rendering — a technique used in games with complex environments, allowing for efficient rendering of large scenes by creating portals to different areas
Level of detail (LOD) management — strategies and techniques for dynamically adjusting object complexity based on distance or other factors to optimize performance
Baked lighting — pre-computed lighting data stored in textures or other formats, used to accelerate real-time lighting calculations
Dynamic occlusion — techniques for updating visibility and occlusion in real-time, often using physics engines or dynamic simulation

knowledge.what_it_is: DirectX is a set of APIs and tools provided by Microsoft for creating games on Windows, including graphics, sound, and input processing. It is widely used in the game development industry due to its performance, flexibility, and ease of use.
what_you_will_learn: Device Objects — managing DirectX devices, including graphics cards and sound cards
Direct3D — creating 2D and 3D graphics using DirectX's graphics API
DirectSound — handling audio processing and playback in games
Input Devices — working with keyboard, mouse, and gamepad input in DirectX applications
Resource Management — loading and managing textures, meshes, and other game assets
Shader Programming — writing shaders for advanced graphics effects using HLSL or GLSL
DirectX Math Library — using the built-in math library for vector and matrix operations
DirectX Debugging Tools — utilizing DirectX's debugging tools for performance optimization

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenGL is a cross-platform, open-standard API (Application Programming Interface) for rendering 2D and 3d graphics on various devices, including desktops, laptops, mobile phones, and game consoles. It provides a low-level interface to hardware-accelerated graphics processing units (GPUs).
what_you_will_learn: OpenGL Context — setting up an OpenGL context for rendering, including creating a window, initializing the GPU, and configuring display settings
Vertex Buffer Objects (VBOs) — storing and managing vertex data in GPU memory for efficient rendering
Index Buffers — using index buffers to reduce vertex count and improve rendering performance
Shaders — writing and compiling shader programs for custom graphics processing, including vertex, fragment, and geometry shaders
Uniforms and Attributes — understanding the difference between uniforms and attributes, and how to use them in OpenGL
Texture Mapping — loading and applying textures to 3D models using OpenGL's texture mapping functionality
Framebuffer Objects (FBOs) — creating and managing framebuffer objects for rendering to off-screen buffers or textures
OpenGL Profiling Tools — using built-in profiling tools, such as glDebugMessageControlEXT, to optimize OpenGL performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Vulkan is a cross-platform, low-level graphics API developed by Khronos Group, designed to provide high-performance and flexibility for game developers. It allows for direct access to GPU resources and supports multi-threading and async compute.
what_you_will_learn: Device creation and management — creating Vulkan instances, devices, and queues
Graphics pipeline setup — setting up vertex and fragment shaders, input assemblies, and render passes
Buffer and image management — allocating and binding buffers, images, and textures
Shader programming — writing vertex and fragment shaders in GLSL or SPIR-V
Command buffer recording — building command buffers for rendering and compute operations
Synchronization primitives — using fences, semaphores, and events for synchronization
Resource allocation and deallocation — managing memory and resources on the GPU

knowledge.what_it_is: Reflection is a rendering technique used to simulate the appearance of reflective surfaces, such as water, glass, or metal, by capturing and displaying the surrounding environment. In game development, reflection is crucial for creating realistic and immersive environments.
what_you_will_learn: Screen Space Ambient Occlusion (SSAO) — a technique for adding depth cues to reflections, simulating ambient occlusion effects in screen space
Cube Mapping — a texture mapping technique used to store and render reflective surfaces, commonly used for water and glass reflections
Reflection Probes — 3D volumes that capture the surrounding environment and are used to generate realistic reflections on reflective surfaces
Screen Space Reflections (SSR) — a technique for rendering reflections in screen space, using techniques like ray marching or screen space ambient occlusion
Environment Mapping — a texture mapping technique used to store and render reflective surfaces, commonly used for metal and glass reflections
Reflection Volumes — 3D volumes that capture the surrounding environment and are used to generate realistic reflections on reflective surfaces
Ray Tracing Reflections — using ray tracing techniques to simulate accurate reflection of light on reflective surfaces

knowledge.what_it_is: Mapping refers to the process of transforming game objects or entities from one coordinate system to another, often used in games with large open worlds or complex level designs. This involves creating a spatial hierarchy and using various techniques such as octrees, k-d trees, or grid-based systems to efficiently manage and query game objects.
what_you_will_learn: Coordinate Systems — understanding the different types of coordinate systems (world, view, object) and how they are used in game development
Spatial Hierarchy — building a spatial hierarchy using data structures such as octrees or k-d trees to efficiently manage and query game objects
Grid-Based Systems — implementing grid-based systems for efficient collision detection and response
Mapping Algorithms — understanding and implementing various mapping algorithms such as barycentric coordinates, spherical harmonics, or texture mapping
Level of Detail (LOD) — using LOD techniques to reduce polygon counts and improve performance in complex scenes
Procedural Mapping — generating terrain or other game environments procedurally using algorithms such as Perlin noise or simplex noise

knowledge.what_it_is: An occluder is a game engine feature that hides or culls objects from view when they are not visible to the player, improving performance and reducing visual clutter. It is commonly used in games with complex environments and large numbers of objects.
what_you_will_learn: Occlusion Culling — techniques for determining which objects are not visible to the camera, including frustum culling, sphere culling, and occluder mesh generation
Portal-based Occlusion — using portals to reduce the number of objects that need to be rendered, improving performance in games with complex environments
Occluder Mesh Generation — algorithms for generating occluder meshes from 3D models, including mesh simplification and optimization techniques
Frustum Culling — determining which objects are within the camera's frustum (viewing volume) and thus visible to the player
Sphere Culling — using spheres to approximate object bounding boxes and improve culling performance in games with large numbers of objects
Occlusion Sorting — sorting objects by their occluder distance or other metrics to optimize rendering order and reduce visual artifacts
Occluder-based Level of Detail (LOD) — using occluders to determine which level of detail to render for each object, based on its distance from the camera

knowledge.what_it_is: Culling is a rendering technique used to reduce the number of objects visible on screen, improving performance and reducing visual clutter. It involves selectively hiding or removing objects that are not currently visible to the player.
what_you_will_learn: Frustum Culling — using the camera's frustum to determine which objects are within view
Sphere Culling — using a sphere centered on the camera to quickly eliminate objects outside its bounds
Occlusion Culling — using other objects or geometry to hide objects that are not visible due to occlusion
Level of Detail (LOD) — dynamically switching between different models or textures based on distance from the camera
Baked Lighting and Shadows — precomputing lighting and shadow data for static objects to reduce dynamic culling overhead
Cull Distance — setting a maximum distance beyond which objects are culled, reducing visual clutter and improving performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Clipping is a technique used in computer graphics to determine which parts of an object are visible and should be rendered, improving performance by reducing unnecessary calculations. In game development, clipping is essential for efficient rendering of complex scenes.
what_you_will_learn: Frustum Culling — using the camera's frustum to quickly eliminate objects outside its view volume
Sphere Clipping — testing if a sphere intersects with other shapes or planes in the scene
Box Clipping — determining if an axis-aligned bounding box (AABB) is within another AABB or not
OBB (Oriented Bounding Box) Clipping — using oriented boxes to enclose complex objects and reduce intersection tests
K-D Tree (k-d tree) Traversal — efficiently traversing the spatial hierarchy of objects in a scene for clipping purposes
BSP (Binary Space Partitioning) Trees — partitioning space into regions to quickly eliminate objects outside the view frustum
Portal Clipping — using portals to reduce the number of objects that need to be clipped and rendered
LOD (Level of Detail) Clipping — dynamically adjusting object complexity based on distance from the camera for efficient rendering

knowledge.what_it_is: Color is a fundamental concept in computer graphics, used to add visual interest and realism to game environments. It involves understanding color theory, color models (RGB, RGBA, CMYK), and color spaces (sRGB, Adobe RGB). In game development, color management is crucial for achieving consistent visuals across different platforms and devices.
what_you_will_learn: Color Models — Understanding the differences between RGB, RGBA, and CMYK color models, including their strengths and limitations
Color Spaces — Familiarity with sRGB, Adobe RGB, and other color spaces used in game development, including their characteristics and applications
RGBA Color Format — Working with 32-bit floating-point colors, including alpha blending and premultiplication
Gamma Correction — Understanding the importance of gamma correction for accurate color representation on different displays
Color Grading — Applying color grading techniques to create a consistent visual style in game environments
Material Colors — Assigning colors to materials using texture maps, normal maps, and other techniques
Lighting Color — Understanding how lighting affects color perception in games, including the use of ambient Occlusion and volumetric lighting

knowledge.what_it_is: Stencil shadowing is a technique used in computer graphics to create accurate and efficient shadows in real-time rendering applications, particularly in games. It works by using a stencil buffer to store information about which pixels are in the shadow region.
what_you_will_learn: Stencil buffer — a buffer that stores binary values (0 or 1) for each pixel, used to determine whether a pixel is in the shadow region
Shadow mapping — a technique that uses a depth map of the scene to determine which pixels are in the shadow region
Depth testing — a process that compares the depth value of two pixels and determines which one is closer to the camera
Stencil operations — a set of instructions that define how the stencil buffer is updated based on the results of depth testing
Shadow acne — an artifact that occurs when the stencil buffer is not properly cleared, causing incorrect shadows to be rendered
Stencil shadow mapping — a technique that uses both stencil and shadow mapping to create accurate and efficient shadows
Multi-sampling anti-aliasing (MSAA) — a technique used to reduce aliasing artifacts in stencil shadows by sampling multiple pixels around the edge of the shadow region

knowledge.what_it_is: Visual perception refers to how humans perceive and interpret visual information, which is crucial for game developers to create immersive and engaging experiences. Understanding human vision and perception helps developers design more realistic graphics, lighting, and user interfaces.
what_you_will_learn: Color models — understanding RGB, RGBA, sRGB, and other color spaces used in game development
Gamma correction — applying non-linear transformations to achieve accurate color representation on different devices
Contrast ratio — calculating the difference between light and dark areas to ensure readability and visual appeal
Field of view (FOV) — adjusting camera angles and perspective to create a sense of immersion and presence
Depth cues — using techniques like linear perspective, atmospheric perspective, and depth maps to create a sense of depth
Motion blur — simulating the blurring effect caused by fast-moving objects or camera motion
Anti-aliasing (AA) — reducing jagged edges and improving image quality through various techniques like MSAA, SSAA, and FXAA
Screen space ambient occlusion (SSAO) — adding depth and realism to scenes with ambient occlusion effects

knowledge.what_it_is: Lighting and shadow are essential components of computer graphics, used to create realistic and immersive environments in games. Lighting simulates the way light interacts with objects in a scene, while shadows add depth and context.
what_you_will_learn: Ambient Occlusion — a technique for simulating ambient lighting effects, such as softening or darkening areas of an object
Diffuse Lighting — calculating how light scatters when hitting a surface, including diffuse reflection and transmission
Specular Lighting — modeling the way light reflects off shiny surfaces, including highlights and specular reflections
Shadow Mapping — rendering shadows by capturing depth information from the scene and using it to determine which areas are in shadow
Volumetric Lighting — simulating the way light interacts with participating media, such as fog or smoke
Light Attenuation — reducing the intensity of light over distance, including linear and quadratic attenuation
Baked Lighting — precomputing lighting information for static objects to improve performance
Real-time Global Illumination (RTGI) — simulating indirect lighting effects in real-time using techniques like spherical harmonics or screen-space ambient occlusion

knowledge.what_it_is: Tone reproduction refers to the process of accurately representing the way light interacts with materials and surfaces in a digital environment, mimicking real-world lighting behavior. This is crucial for creating realistic and immersive game experiences.
what_you_will_learn: Lambertian reflectance — modeling diffuse reflection using the Lambertian reflectance model, accounting for surface roughness and normal distribution
Blinn-Phong shading — implementing specular highlights using the Blinn-Phong shading model, incorporating factors like material smoothness and light intensity
Mipmap generation — creating mipmaps to reduce texture memory usage while maintaining visual quality, essential for real-time rendering
Tone mapping — applying tone mapping techniques (e.g., Reinhard, Mantiuk) to adapt high dynamic range images to the display's limited dynamic range
Screen space ambient occlusion (SSAO) — implementing SSAO to simulate ambient occlusion in screen space, enhancing scene depth and realism
Light probes — using light probes to capture and store lighting information from a scene, allowing for more accurate indirect lighting calculations
Spherical harmonics (SH) — representing complex lighting environments using SH coefficients, enabling efficient and accurate lighting calculations

knowledge.what_it_is: A shadow map is a technique used in computer graphics to accelerate rendering of complex scenes by precomputing and storing the shadows of light sources on a 2D texture, allowing for fast lookup during rendering.
what_you_will_learn: Shadow mapping — a technique for accelerating shadow calculation using a 2D texture
Depth buffer — a data structure used to store depth values for each pixel in the scene
Light space perspective — transforming world coordinates into light space to simplify calculations
Shadow matrix — a transformation matrix used to map world coordinates into light space
Biasing techniques — methods for reducing artifacts and improving shadow quality, such as biasing, anisotropic filtering, and texture-based methods
Shadow resolution — the size of the 2D texture used to store shadows, affecting trade-offs between quality and performance
Shadow mapping algorithms — various approaches for generating and updating shadow maps, including screen-space ambient occlusion (SSAO) and cascaded shadow maps
Shadow caching — techniques for reducing the overhead of recalculating shadows, such as using precomputed shadows or texture atlasing

knowledge.what_it_is: A light source is an essential component of computer graphics, responsible for simulating real-world lighting effects in games. It can be used to create realistic environments, enhance gameplay experience, and add visual depth to game worlds.
what_you_will_learn: Point Light — a basic type of light source that emits light from a single point in 3D space
Directional Light — simulates real-world sunlight by emitting light from an infinite distance, often used for outdoor environments
Spot Light — a directional light with a cone-shaped beam, commonly used to create focused lighting effects like spotlights or headlights
Area Light — simulates the effect of a physical light source, such as a lamp or a window, and is often used in indoor environments
Light Attenuation — controlling how light intensity decreases over distance, essential for creating realistic lighting effects
Light Mapping — pre-computing lighting information and storing it in textures to improve rendering performance
Shadow Maps — using depth buffers to render shadows in real-time, a common technique used in modern games

knowledge.what_it_is: Computer animation refers to the process of generating motion graphics using computer software, commonly used in game development to create realistic character movements, environments, and special effects.
what_you_will_learn: Keyframe animation — creating a sequence of keyframes that define the start and end points of an animation, with intermediate frames interpolated by the animation system
Physics-based animation — simulating real-world physics to create realistic animations, using techniques such as rigid body dynamics, soft body simulations, and fluid dynamics
Motion capture — recording human or animal movements using specialized equipment, and then transferring those movements to a digital character
Animation blending — combining multiple animations to create more complex movements, such as walking and running
Inverse kinematics — calculating the joint angles of a character's skeleton to achieve a desired pose or movement
Skinning — assigning 3D models to a character's skeleton, allowing for realistic deformation and animation
Rigging — creating a virtual skeleton for a 3D model, used for animation and posing
Animation state machines — designing systems that manage complex animations, such as walking, running, or jumping

knowledge.what_it_is: A cube is a three-dimensional geometric shape with six square faces, used extensively in game development for various purposes such as collision detection, level design, and visual representation. It is a fundamental concept in computer graphics and is often used as a building block for more complex shapes.
what_you_will_learn: Vertex Buffer Object (VBO) — storing cube vertices in GPU memory for efficient rendering
Index Buffer Object (IBO) — storing cube indices to reduce vertex duplication and improve performance
Cube Mapping — using six faces of a cube as a texture map for environment mapping and reflections
Octree — partitioning the 3D space into octants to efficiently detect collisions between cubes and other objects
BSP Tree (Binary Space Partitioning) — recursively dividing space into two sub-spaces to optimize collision detection and rendering
Cube-based Level Design — using cubes as building blocks for creating complex level structures and environments
Frustum Culling — using the cube's bounding box to determine whether it is visible within the camera's frustum and thus needs to be rendered

knowledge.what_it_is: Directional lighting refers to the simulation of real-world light sources, such as lamps or sunlight, in a game environment. This technique is used to create more realistic and immersive lighting effects.
what_you_will_learn: Vector Math — understanding how to represent and manipulate vectors for directional lighting calculations
Light Source Types — learning about the different types of light sources used in games, including point lights, spot lights, and directional lights
Light Attenuation — implementing techniques to simulate the decrease in light intensity as it travels through a scene
Shadow Mapping — using shadow maps to efficiently render shadows for complex scenes
Normal Mapping — applying normal mapping techniques to enhance surface detail and realism
Specular Lighting — understanding how to calculate and apply specular lighting effects for realistic reflections
Light Probes — learning about the use of light probes to capture and store real-world lighting environments
Real-time Global Illumination — implementing techniques such as screen-space ambient occlusion (SSAO) or volumetric rendering for more accurate global illumination

knowledge.what_it_is: A spot light is a type of directional light source used to create highlights and accents in game environments, often used to illuminate specific objects or areas.
what_you_will_learn: Spot light types — understanding the different types of spot lights (e.g. spherical, rectangular) and their applications
Light falloff — learning about the various light falloff models (e.g. linear, quadratic, inverse square) and how to implement them in game engines
Shadow mapping — understanding the basics of shadow mapping and its implementation in game engines, including techniques for reducing artifacts
Spot light attenuation — learning about the different types of spot light attenuation (e.g. cosine, sine) and their effects on lighting
Light clustering — understanding how to optimize spot lights using light clustering techniques to reduce performance overhead
Spot light baking — learning about the process of baking spot lights into static textures or normal maps for improved performance
Real-time vs precomputed lighting — understanding the trade-offs between real-time and precomputed lighting techniques in game development

knowledge.what_it_is: Cascaded shadows are a technique used to improve shadow quality and reduce artifacts in real-time rendering, particularly in games with complex environments.
what_you_will_learn: Cascaded Shadow Maps — a method for rendering high-quality shadows by dividing the screen into multiple regions, each with its own shadow map
Shadow Cascades — a hierarchical approach to rendering shadows, where more detailed shadow maps are used in areas closer to the light source
Cascade Levels — defining multiple levels of detail for shadow maps based on distance from the camera or light source
Shadow Atlas — a texture that stores multiple shadow maps, reducing memory usage and improving performance
Cascaded Shadow Mapping with Depth Buffer — using the depth buffer to improve shadow accuracy and reduce artifacts
Multi-Resolution Cascaded Shadows — rendering shadows at different resolutions based on distance from the camera or light source

knowledge.what_it_is: A point is a fundamental concept in computer graphics, representing a location in 2D or 3D space with precise coordinates. It is used extensively in game development for tasks such as collision detection, physics simulations, and rendering.
what_you_will_learn: Vector representation — understanding how points are represented using vectors (e.g., 2D/3D vector math) and their applications
Point-in-polygon testing — algorithms for determining whether a point lies within or outside a polygon (e.g., ray casting, winding number)
Distance calculations — computing distances between points in Euclidean space (e.g., Manhattan distance, Minkowski distance)
Barycentric coordinates — using barycentric weights to compute the position of a point inside a triangle
Point sampling — techniques for selecting representative points from a set of data (e.g., stratified sampling, importance sampling)
Collision detection with points — algorithms for detecting collisions between points and other geometric shapes (e.g., spheres, boxes)
Point cloud processing — methods for filtering, simplifying, and visualizing large point clouds
GPU acceleration for point operations — leveraging GPU capabilities to accelerate point-related computations

knowledge.what_it_is: Infinite rendering refers to the ability of a game engine or graphics pipeline to render an infinite number of objects, such as trees, rocks, or buildings, without running out of memory or performance. This is achieved through various techniques like instancing, level of detail (LOD), and occlusion culling.
what_you_will_learn: Instancing — rendering multiple instances of the same object with a single draw call, using techniques like vertex buffer objects (VBOs) and transform feedback
Level of Detail (LOD) — automatically switching between different mesh resolutions based on distance from camera or other factors to reduce polygon count and improve performance
Occlusion Culling — dynamically removing objects that are not visible due to occlusion by other objects, using techniques like frustum culling and occlusion queries
Billboarding — rendering 2D sprites as billboards to represent complex geometry at a distance, often used for foliage or water effects
Procedural Generation — generating terrain, buildings, or other environments on the fly using algorithms and mathematical formulas, rather than loading pre-made assets

knowledge.what_it_is: A diffuse term refers to the way light scatters in various directions when hitting a surface, creating a soft and even illumination effect. In game development, diffuse lighting is used to add realism to 3D environments.
what_you_will_learn: Diffuse BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) — understanding how light interacts with surfaces, including Lambertian reflection and diffuse scattering
Cook-Torrance model — a physically-based diffuse BRDF that models the way light scatters on rough surfaces, used in many game engines
GGX (Smith-Gamma) specular-diffuse separation — a technique for separating diffuse and specular components of a material's reflectance function
Normal mapping — using texture coordinates to simulate detailed surface geometry, often combined with diffuse textures
Diffuse texture baking — pre-computing and storing diffuse textures on 3D models to reduce rendering overhead
Spherical harmonics (SH) lighting — representing complex lighting environments as a sum of spherical harmonic basis functions, used for efficient diffuse lighting in games

knowledge.what_it_is: In computer graphics, Specular refers to the shiny reflection of light on surfaces, creating highlights and adding realism to game environments.
what_you_will_learn: Cook-Torrance BRDF — a physically-based model for simulating specular reflections in real-time rendering
GGX (Glossy Microfacet Model) — a widely-used approximation of the Cook-Torrance BRDF, suitable for most game engines and platforms
Marmoset's Specular Lighting — an advanced technique for creating realistic specular highlights using normal maps and texture blending
SSS (Subsurface Scattering) — simulating light transport through translucent materials like skin, glass, or wax to create more realistic specular effects
Specular Occlusion — a technique for reducing the visibility of specular highlights on surfaces with complex geometry or high-frequency details
Normal Mapping and Specular Highlights — how to use normal maps to enhance surface detail and create more convincing specular reflections
Physically-Based Rendering (PBR) and Specular Materials — understanding how PBR materials work and how to create realistic specular effects using them

knowledge.what_it_is: A bump map is a 3D texture that simulates surface details such as bumps, dents, and scratches on a game object's surface. It is used to add visual detail and realism to 3D models without increasing polygon count.
what_you_will_learn: Bump mapping — the process of applying a bump map to a game object's surface to simulate surface details
Normal maps — high-resolution texture maps that store normal vector data for each pixel, used in conjunction with bump mapping
Height maps — 2D or 3D textures that store height information for each pixel, used as input for bump mapping algorithms
Bump mapping techniques — including per-pixel bump mapping, multitexturing, and screen-space ambient occlusion (SSAO)
Bump map resolution and filtering — understanding the trade-offs between texture resolution, mipmapping, and anisotropic filtering
Bump mapping in game engines — how to implement bump mapping using popular game engines such as Unity and Unreal Engine
Bump map creation tools — including 3D modeling software like Blender and Maya, and texture editing software like Adobe Photoshop

knowledge.what_it_is: Parallax is an effect used in game development to create depth and visual interest by moving background elements at a different speed than foreground elements, giving the illusion of distance.
what_you_will_learn: Camera layering — understanding how to position multiple cameras with different parallax settings to achieve realistic depth effects
Background spritesheet management — techniques for optimizing and animating large background images using sprite sheets and texture atlasing
Parallax scrolling — implementing smooth, seamless scrolling of background elements using delta calculations and interpolation
Depth sorting — understanding how to correctly sort game objects based on their distance from the camera to prevent visual artifacts
Layering and masking — techniques for creating complex layering effects using render textures, depth buffers, and stencil masks
Atmospheric effects — implementing realistic atmospheric effects such as fog, haze, and god rays using shaders and post-processing techniques

knowledge.what_it_is: Textures are 2D or 3D images used to add visual detail and realism to game environments, characters, and objects. They can be applied using various techniques such as texture mapping, normal mapping, and ambient occlusion.
what_you_will_learn: Texture types — understanding the differences between diffuse textures, specular textures, normal textures, and height textures
Texture formats — learning about popular formats like PNG, JPEG, DDS, and KTX2, including their strengths and weaknesses
Texture compression — techniques for reducing texture size without sacrificing quality, such as DXT, ETC, and ASTC
Texture filtering — understanding the importance of texture filtering in game rendering, including bilinear, trilinear, and anisotropic filtering
Texture atlasing — optimizing texture usage by combining multiple textures into a single atlas image
Texture streaming — techniques for loading and unloading textures dynamically during gameplay to reduce memory usage
Normal mapping — creating detailed normal maps from high-poly 3D models or low-resolution textures
Ambient occlusion — adding depth and realism with ambient occlusion, including techniques like screen-space AO and object-space AO

knowledge.what_it_is: Fog, also known as fog of war or fog of battle, is an environmental effect used to obscure the player's view and create a sense of mystery in games. It can be implemented using various techniques such as depth-based fog, texture-based fog, or screen-space ambient occlusion.
what_you_will_learn: Fog types — understanding the different types of fog (density, color, height) and how to implement them
Depth-based fog — using the depth buffer to render fog based on distance from the camera
Texture-based fog — using a texture map to create realistic fog effects
Screen-space ambient occlusion — using screen space techniques to simulate ambient occlusion in foggy environments
Fog rendering pipeline — understanding how to integrate fog into the game's graphics pipeline, including shader modifications and texture sampling
Fog density calculation — learning how to calculate fog density based on environmental factors such as humidity, temperature, and wind speed

knowledge.what_it_is: Shadows are a fundamental aspect of computer graphics, used to create depth and realism in game environments. They can be created using various techniques such as shadow mapping, shadow volumes, or screen-space ambient occlusion.
what_you_will_learn: Shadow Mapping — rendering scene geometry into a texture map for later use in the main pass
Shadow Volumes — creating a 3D representation of shadows by storing and manipulating volume data
Screen-Space Ambient Occlusion (SSAO) — using screen-space techniques to simulate ambient occlusion and add depth to scenes
Point Light Shadows — simulating point light sources with accurate shadowing, including penumbra and umbra regions
Directional Light Shadows — simulating directional light sources with accurate shadowing, including soft shadows and edge cases
Shadow Culling — optimizing shadow rendering by culling unnecessary geometry or using occlusion queries
Shadow Atlas — storing multiple shadow maps in a single texture for efficient rendering
Shadow Resolution Scaling (SRS) — improving shadow quality by upscaling the resolution of shadow maps

knowledge.what_it_is: The horizon in computer graphics refers to the apparent boundary between the sky and the earth, used in game development to create realistic environments and simulate atmospheric effects.
what_you_will_learn: Plane equations — representing horizon geometry using mathematical formulas (e.g., ax + by + cz = d)
Frustum culling — optimizing rendering performance by only drawing objects within the camera's view frustum, including horizon-based clipping planes
Skybox textures — creating and applying 360-degree skybox textures to simulate atmospheric effects, such as sun position, cloud density, and misting
Atmospheric scattering — simulating light scattering in the atmosphere using techniques like Rayleigh or Mie scattering, affecting color and intensity of horizon objects
Heightmap generation — creating heightmaps for terrain rendering, including horizon-based algorithms for generating realistic elevation maps
Horizon-based occlusion — using horizon geometry to determine which objects are visible and should be rendered, improving performance in complex scenes

knowledge.what_it_is: A frustum is a 3D geometric shape formed by truncating a pyramid or cone at its apex, resulting in a square or rectangular base and a smaller polygonal top. In computer graphics, it's used to represent the view volume of a camera.
what_you_will_learn: Frustum culling — using spatial partitioning to quickly eliminate objects outside the frustum, improving rendering performance
Frustum planes — defining the six planes that make up the frustum (near, far, left, right, top, bottom)
Perspective division — transforming 3D points into screen space by dividing by the w-component of the homogeneous coordinates
Frustum intersection — determining if a ray or triangle intersects with the frustum, used for occlusion culling and visibility determination
View-frustum clipping — removing objects outside the camera's view volume to improve rendering efficiency
Frustum-based occlusion culling — using the frustum to quickly eliminate objects that are not visible to the camera
Frustum transformation matrices — creating matrices to transform points from world space to frustum space and vice versa

knowledge.what_it_is: A polygon is a two-dimensional shape with at least three sides, used extensively in game development for collision detection, level design, and rendering. In computer graphics, polygons are often referred to as polytopes.
what_you_will_learn: Polygon types — understanding the differences between convex, concave, and self-intersecting polygons
Polygon mesh generation — creating polygon meshes from 3D models using tools like Blender or Maya
Polygon rendering — optimizing polygon rendering for better performance in games using techniques like level of detail (LOD) and occlusion culling
Collision detection with polygons — implementing collision detection between polygons using algorithms like Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)
Polygon clipping — understanding how to clip polygons against other shapes or planes for efficient rendering
Polygon tessellation — subdividing complex polygons into simpler ones for better rendering performance
Polygon triangulation — converting non-triangular polygons to triangular meshes for easier rendering and collision detection

knowledge.what_it_is: A polyhedron is a three-dimensional shape with flat polygonal faces, edges, and vertices. In game development, polyhedrons are used to create complex 3D models, environments, and characters.
what_you_will_learn: Polygon mesh — representing 3D objects as collections of connected polygons for rendering
Vertex buffer object (VBO) — storing vertex data in GPU memory for efficient rendering
Index buffer object (IBO) — storing index data in GPU memory to accelerate polygon rendering
Normal mapping — using texture coordinates to simulate detailed surface normal information
Tangent space — transforming normals and tangents from model space to world space for accurate lighting
Baked lighting — precomputing lighting effects on static objects to reduce dynamic lighting overhead
Dynamic meshing — generating or updating polygon meshes in real-time based on game state or user input

knowledge.what_it_is: Lighting in game development refers to the simulation of real-world lighting effects, including ambient occlusion, shadows, and reflections, to create a visually appealing and immersive environment. This involves understanding the physics of light, its interaction with various materials, and how it affects gameplay.
what_you_will_learn: Types of lights — Point Light, Directional Light, Spot Light, Ambient Occlusion, Sun Light
Light attenuation — understanding how light intensity decreases with distance, including linear, quadratic, and exponential falloff
Shadows — basic concepts, including shadow mapping, shadow volumes, and cascaded shadows
Light mapping — techniques for pre-computing lighting information, including static and dynamic light maps
Baked lighting — understanding the trade-offs between baked lighting, real-time lighting, and dynamic lighting
Light transport — simulating how light interacts with objects, including diffuse, specular, and ambient occlusion
GI (Global Illumination) techniques — basics of GI, including Spherical Harmonics, Radiance Transfer, and Light Propagation Volumes

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenGL ES (Embedded Systems) is an open-source cross-platform API for rendering 2D and 3D graphics on mobile devices, embedded systems, and other platforms. It provides a standardized interface for accessing graphics hardware and is widely used in game development.
what_you_will_learn: Vertex Buffer Objects (VBOs) — storing and managing vertex data in GPU memory
Index Buffer Objects (IBOs) — optimizing rendering with index buffers and reducing draw calls
Texture Mapping Units (TMUs) — using multiple texture units for complex materials and effects
Fragment Shaders — writing custom shaders for pixel-level operations, lighting, and texturing
OpenGL ES 3.0+ features: multi-threading, async compute, and improved graphics capabilities
EGL (Embedded System Graphics Library) integration — using EGL to manage OpenGL ES contexts and surfaces
OpenGL ES extensions: ETC1/ETC2 compression, PVRTC texture compression, and other platform-specific features

knowledge.what_it_is: WebGL (Web Graphics Library) is an API for rendering interactive 2D and 3D graphics within any compatible web browser without the use of plug-ins. It's widely used in game development, scientific visualization, and other fields where high-performance graphics are required.
what_you_will_learn: Shaders — writing custom GLSL code for vertex and fragment shaders to manipulate graphics
Buffer Objects — using GPU memory to store and manage large datasets, such as mesh vertices and indices
Textures — loading and manipulating image data in WebGL, including texture coordinates and filtering
Framebuffers — rendering to off-screen buffers for effects like depth of field, motion blur, or ambient occlusion
WebGL 2.0 — understanding the new features and improvements introduced in the latest version of the API
WebGL Extensions — using community-maintained extensions to add support for advanced graphics features, such as multi-threading or physics-based rendering
WebGL Debugging Tools — using browser dev tools and external libraries like WebGL Inspector to debug and optimize your code

knowledge.what_it_is: Metal is a low-overhead, high-performance graphics API developed by Apple, designed for use on iOS and macOS devices. It provides direct access to the GPU, allowing developers to optimize their games for maximum performance.
what_you_will_learn: MTLDevice — creating a Metal device object to manage resources and execute commands
MTLCommandQueue — managing command buffers and submitting them to the GPU
MTLBuffer — creating vertex and index buffers for data storage and transfer
MTLTexture — creating textures for 2D and 3D rendering, including support for mipmapping and multisampling
MTLLibrary — loading shader code and compiling it into Metal kernel functions
MTLFunction — executing Metal kernels on the GPU, with support for compute shaders and graphics pipelines
Metal Shading Language (MSL) — writing custom shaders using a C-like syntax
Buffer Objects vs. Texture Objects — understanding when to use each type of resource

knowledge.what_it_is: Movement refers to the ability of game entities, such as characters or objects, to change their position and orientation within a virtual environment. This involves calculating and updating the entity's velocity, acceleration, and rotation based on user input, physics, and other factors.
what_you_will_learn: Vector math — using 2D/3D vectors for position, velocity, and acceleration calculations
Linear interpolation — smoothly transitioning between two positions or orientations
Quaternions — representing rotations in 3D space with minimal loss of precision
Euler angles — converting between quaternions and Euler angle representations
Physics engines — integrating popular libraries like PhysX, Havok, or Bullet for realistic simulations
Collision detection — using spatial data structures (e.g., octrees) to efficiently detect collisions
Pathfinding algorithms — implementing A*, Dijkstra's algorithm, or other techniques for finding optimal paths
Motion planning — generating smooth and collision-free motion trajectories
subtopics: collision detection data structures (e.g., octrees, k-d trees)
pathfinding algorithm implementations (e.g., a*, dijkstra's algorithm)

knowledge.what_it_is: Decision making in game development refers to the process of creating algorithms that enable non-player characters (NPCs) or agents to make decisions based on their environment, goals, and constraints. This is crucial for creating realistic and engaging gameplay experiences.
what_you_will_learn: Finite State Machines — a simple, easy-to-implement decision-making model using states and transitions
Behavior Trees — a more complex decision-making model that uses nodes to represent tasks and conditions
Decision Trees — a tree-like structure used for classification and regression tasks in game AI
Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) — a mathematical framework for modeling decision-making under uncertainty
Q-learning — a type of reinforcement learning algorithm used for training agents to make decisions based on rewards
Sarsa — an online variant of Q-learning that updates the action-value function as experience is gathered
Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) — using neural networks to learn decision-making policies in complex environments

knowledge.what_it_is: A Naive Bayes classifier is a type of probabilistic machine learning algorithm used to classify game objects or events based on their features. It assumes independence between features and uses Bayes' theorem to calculate probabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Bayesian inference — updating probability distributions using Bayes' theorem
Gaussian distribution — modeling continuous feature data with mean and variance parameters
Multinomial distribution — modeling categorical feature data with probability mass function
Naive Bayes assumption — independence between features, simplifying calculation of conditional probabilities
Feature engineering — selecting relevant game-related features for classification (e.g., player movement, score)
Hyperparameter tuning — optimizing Naive Bayes model parameters using techniques like grid search or cross-validation
Class balancing — handling class imbalance by oversampling minority classes or undersampling majority classes

knowledge.what_it_is: A decision tree is a type of machine learning model used in game development to make decisions based on input data, often used for pathfinding, resource allocation, and player behavior prediction. It's a popular choice due to its simplicity and interpretability.
what_you_will_learn: Decision Tree Algorithm — the basic concept of decision trees, including how they work and their advantages
Game State Representation — representing game state as input features for the decision tree model
Feature Engineering — selecting relevant features from game data to improve decision tree accuracy
Splitting Criteria — understanding different splitting criteria (e.g., Gini impurity, information gain) and how they affect decision trees
Pruning Decision Trees — techniques for pruning decision trees to prevent overfitting and improve generalization
Ensemble Methods — combining multiple decision trees using methods like bagging or boosting for improved accuracy

knowledge.what_it_is: Decision learning is an AI technique used to enable game agents to make decisions based on observations and experiences, allowing them to adapt to changing game environments.
what_you_will_learn: Q-learning — a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm for updating action-value functions in game environments
SARSA — a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that updates the action-value function using the current state and next state
Deep Q-Networks (DQN) — a type of neural network used to approximate the action-value function in complex game environments
Policy Gradient Methods — a class of algorithms for learning policies directly from experience, without requiring an explicit value function
Actor-Critic Methods — a combination of policy gradient methods and value function estimation, allowing agents to balance exploration and exploitation
Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) — a planning algorithm that uses tree search and Monte Carlo simulations to make decisions in complex game environments
Decision Trees — a type of machine learning model used to represent decision-making policies in games
Neural Decision Processes (NDPs) — a class of algorithms that combine neural networks with decision processes to enable agents to adapt to changing game environments

knowledge.what_it_is: A board game is a type of tabletop game where players interact with a physical or virtual game board, using rules and mechanics to achieve a goal. In game development, creating a board game involves designing and implementing the game's core mechanics, user interface, and AI.
what_you_will_learn: Game states — managing the game's current state, including player positions, turn counters, and game objects
Board representation — using data structures like grids or graphs to represent the game board, with considerations for performance and memory usage
Player movement — implementing algorithms for player movement, such as pathfinding or grid-based navigation
Game rules engine — designing a system to enforce game rules, including validation, resolution, and notification of rule-breaking events
AI decision-making — creating algorithms for AI-controlled players, including decision trees, minimax, or reinforcement learning
User interface design — crafting an intuitive and engaging UI for human players, with considerations for visual feedback, input handling, and game state display
subtopics: json or xml data exchange formats
validation_keywords: json or xml data exchange formats

knowledge.what_it_is: A state machine is a mathematical model used to design and implement game logic, particularly for finite-state machines. It's a crucial concept in game development, enabling developers to create complex behaviors with manageable complexity.
what_you_will_learn: Finite State Machines (FSMs) — modeling game states as nodes in a graph, transitioning between them based on events
State Machine Design Patterns — using techniques like Hierarchical FSMs and Composite FSMs for scalable state machine implementation
Transition Functions — defining the rules for moving from one state to another, including conditional logic and event handling
Epsilon Transitions — allowing for empty transitions between states, useful for modeling asynchronous events
State Machines in Game Engines — integrating state machines with popular game engines like Unity and Unreal Engine using C# or Blueprints
Behavior Trees vs. State Machines — understanding the trade-offs between these two approaches to game logic implementation

knowledge.what_it_is: Reinforcements learning is a type of machine learning used in game development to enable agents to learn from other agents' experiences, improving their decision-making and behavior. It's commonly used in multi-agent systems, such as games with multiple NPCs.
what_you_will_learn: Q-learning — an off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm for updating action-value functions
Deep Q-Networks (DQN) — a type of neural network used to approximate the action-value function in Q-learning
Policy Gradient Methods — a class of on-policy reinforcement learning algorithms for optimizing policies directly
Actor-Critic Methods — a combination of policy gradient methods and value function estimation, used in deep reinforcement learning
Experience Replay — a technique for improving sample efficiency by storing and reusing past experiences
Exploration-Exploitation Trade-off — the fundamental problem in reinforcement learning, where agents must balance exploring new actions with exploiting known good ones

knowledge.what_it_is: Minimax is an algorithm used to make optimal decisions in games with perfect information, such as chess or checkers. It works by simulating all possible moves and their outcomes, evaluating each one based on a heuristic function, and choosing the move that maximizes the chances of winning.
what_you_will_learn: Game tree — representing the game state and possible moves in a tree-like structure
Node evaluation — assigning a score to each node in the game tree based on its position and potential outcomes
Alpha-beta pruning — optimizing the minimax algorithm by pruning branches that will not affect the final decision
Heuristic functions — using domain-specific knowledge to guide the search for optimal moves, such as material imbalance or pawn structure
Transposition tables — storing previously evaluated positions to avoid redundant calculations and improve performance
Iterative deepening — gradually increasing the depth of the minimax search to find the best move without exceeding a time limit
subtopics: minimax with alpha-beta pruning in python

knowledge.what_it_is: A Behavior Tree (BT) is a decision-making algorithm used in game development to manage complex behaviors and actions of non-player characters (NPCs). It's a hierarchical, tree-like structure that represents a series of tasks or actions that need to be performed.
what_you_will_learn: Behavior Tree nodes — understanding the different types of nodes: selectors, actions, decorators, and conditions
Selector nodes — combining multiple child behaviors using logical operators (AND, OR, NOT)
Action nodes — performing a specific action or task, such as moving to a location or attacking an enemy
Decorator nodes — modifying the behavior of child nodes, such as repeating or skipping actions
Condition nodes — evaluating conditions or states before allowing the behavior to proceed
Behavior Tree implementation in C++/C# — using libraries like BehaviorTree.NET or BTSharp for .NET/C#
Behavior Tree implementation in Python — using libraries like PyBT or BT-Builder
Integrating Behavior Trees with game engines like Unity or Unreal Engine

knowledge.what_it_is: Decision Tree Learning is a supervised machine learning algorithm used to classify game states or predict player behavior, by recursively partitioning the data into subsets based on feature values.
what_you_will_learn: ID3 Algorithm — the original decision tree induction algorithm, now considered outdated due to its simplicity and lack of handling missing values
C4.5 Algorithm — an extension of ID3 that handles missing values and uses pruning to improve accuracy
Decision Tree Induction — the process of creating a decision tree from a dataset, using techniques like entropy reduction or information gain
Game State Features — selecting relevant features for decision trees, such as player position, velocity, score, or game events
Tree Pruning — techniques like pre-pruning, post-pruning, or reduced-error pruning to prevent overfitting and improve generalization
Decision Tree Ensembles — combining multiple decision trees using methods like bagging or boosting for improved accuracy and robustness
Game AI Evaluation Metrics — understanding metrics like precision, recall, F1-score, or AUC-ROC to evaluate decision tree performance in game environments

knowledge.what_it_is: Learning is the process of acquiring new knowledge, skills, and experiences to improve game development abilities. It involves understanding game development concepts, techniques, and best practices, as well as staying up-to-date with industry trends and advancements.
what_you_will_learn: Game Development Fundamentals — understanding game architecture, design patterns, and software engineering principles
Learning Management Systems (LMS) — using tools like Udemy, Coursera, or LinkedIn Learning to access online courses and tutorials
Content Creation Pipelines — setting up efficient workflows for creating and managing game assets, levels, and scripts
Version Control Systems (VCS) — utilizing Git, SVN, or Mercurial to manage code changes and collaborate with team members
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) — implementing automated testing, building, and deployment processes using tools like Jenkins, Travis CI, or CircleCI
Game Development Frameworks — understanding popular frameworks like Unity, Unreal Engine, or Godot, including their strengths, weaknesses, and use cases
Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in Games — applying AI and ML techniques to create dynamic game worlds, NPCs, and player experiences

knowledge.what_it_is: AB Pruning is an optimization technique used in game development to reduce the number of nodes evaluated during Alpha-Beta pruning, a popular algorithm for decision-making in games. It is particularly useful for large game trees and can significantly improve gameplay performance.
what_you_will_learn: Alpha-Beta pruning — understanding the basic algorithm and its application in game decision-making
Node evaluation order — learning how to reorder node evaluations to reduce computation time
Transposition table — implementing a hash table to store previously evaluated positions and avoid redundant calculations
Iterative deepening — using a depth-first search with iterative deepening to find the best move without exploring all possible moves
Aspiration levels — setting upper and lower bounds for the evaluation function to prune branches that are unlikely to improve the game state
Null window — understanding how to use the null window to further prune the game tree by eliminating nodes that cannot lead to a win or loss
Zobrist hashing — using a hash function to efficiently store and retrieve transposition table entries
Pruning heuristics — learning various heuristics for pruning branches, such as the minimum-maximum heuristic

knowledge.what_it_is: Fuzzy logic is an extension of classical logic that allows for reasoning with uncertain or imprecise information, often used in game development to model real-world phenomena and make decisions based on incomplete data.
what_you_will_learn: Membership functions — defining the degree of membership of a value in a fuzzy set (e.g., temperature, health)
Fuzzy rules — using IF-THEN statements to combine membership values and determine output (e.g., 'if player is close, then chase')
Defuzzification methods — converting fuzzy outputs back into crisp values for decision-making (e.g., centroid, mean of max)
Type-1 vs. Type-2 fuzzy logic — understanding the differences between these two approaches to fuzzy reasoning and when to use each
Fuzzy clustering algorithms — using techniques like c-means or k-means to group similar game objects or behaviors
Fuzzy decision trees — combining fuzzy rules with decision tree structures for more complex decision-making
Fuzzy neural networks — integrating fuzzy logic with neural network architectures for hybrid intelligence

knowledge.what_it_is: Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses neural networks with multiple layers to learn complex patterns and relationships in data, particularly in image and video processing. In game development, deep learning is used for tasks such as character animation, physics-based rendering, and AI decision-making.
what_you_will_learn: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) — a type of neural network designed specifically for image and video data, commonly used in games for tasks like texture recognition and object detection
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) — a type of neural network well-suited for sequential data such as game state, player input, or animation trajectories
Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks — a variant of RNNs that can learn long-term dependencies in data, used in games for tasks like character animation and dialogue systems
Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) — a type of neural network that can generate new game content such as textures, models, or animations, based on existing data
Transfer learning — the process of using pre-trained deep learning models as a starting point for your own game development tasks, saving time and computational resources
PyTorch — an open-source machine learning library widely used in game development for building and training neural networks, including CNNs and RNNs
TensorFlow — another popular open-source machine learning library used in game development for tasks like deep learning and computer vision
Keras — a high-level neural network API that can run on top of PyTorch or TensorFlow, making it easier to build and train complex models

knowledge.what_it_is: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is a popular game-playing algorithm used in various board games, including Go and Poker. It's a tree search method that uses random sampling to efficiently explore the game tree, making it suitable for real-time applications like video games.
what_you_will_learn: Game tree representation — constructing a tree data structure to represent the game state, with nodes representing states and edges representing actions
Node selection — choosing which node to expand next using heuristics such as Upper Confidence Tree (UCT) or Probability of Improvement (PI)
Expansion — generating child nodes by applying possible moves from the current node, considering factors like move ordering and pruning
Simulation — rolling out a random game from the expanded node, evaluating its outcome using a heuristic function like UCT or PI
Backpropagation — updating the parent node's value based on the simulation result, using techniques like Monte Carlo Counterfactual Regret Minimization (MCCFR)
Tree policy improvement — iteratively improving the tree policy by selecting the best action at each node, leading to more informed exploration and exploitation
Action selection — choosing which action to take in a given state based on the learned policy, considering factors like move ordering and lookahead
Game-specific adaptations — applying MCTS to specific games, such as adapting the algorithm for turn-based or real-time games

knowledge.what_it_is: A Markov system is a mathematical model used to generate random sequences, such as game states or NPC behaviors, based on probability distributions and transition matrices. It's commonly used in games to create realistic simulations of complex systems, like weather or crowd movements.
what_you_will_learn: Markov Chain — a sequence of states where the next state depends only on the current state
Transition Matrix — a matrix that defines the probabilities of moving from one state to another
Stationary Distribution — a probability distribution over all possible states, used to initialize the Markov chain
Ergodicity — the property of a Markov chain where it's possible to reach any state from any other state
Markov Decision Process (MDP) — an extension of the Markov chain that incorporates rewards and actions
Q-learning — a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm used to train MDPs
Value Iteration — a method for solving MDPs by iteratively updating the value function
Policy Gradient Methods — a class of algorithms that learn the optimal policy directly from experience

knowledge.what_it_is: An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is a machine learning model inspired by the human brain's neural structure, used to train agents and NPCs in games. It consists of interconnected nodes (neurons) that process inputs and produce outputs.
what_you_will_learn: Feedforward networks — basic architecture for simple tasks like image classification or game state prediction
Backpropagation — training algorithm for adjusting weights and biases to minimize error between predicted and actual outcomes
Activation functions (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) — introducing non-linearity in neural network outputs to enable complex decision-making
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) — applying convolutional and pooling layers for image recognition tasks like character or object detection
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) — modeling sequential data like game state transitions, player input, or NPC behavior
Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) cells — a type of RNN cell that handles long-term dependencies in sequential data
Neural Network Architectures for Games (e.g., Deep Q-Networks, Policy Gradient Methods)
Training and optimization techniques (e.g., Adam optimizer, batch normalization)

knowledge.what_it_is: Goal-Oriented Behavior (GOB) is a decision-making framework used in game development to create autonomous characters that can achieve complex goals. It's based on the idea of assigning high-level objectives to agents, which are then broken down into smaller tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Goal Trees — hierarchical representation of objectives and sub-objectives
Task Networks — graph-based models for planning and execution
Utility Functions — mathematical functions that assign scores to actions based on their contribution to the goal
Heuristics — rules-of-thumb used to guide decision-making when exact solutions are not feasible
Goal-Action Graphs — data structures that represent the relationship between goals and available actions
Planning Algorithms — techniques for finding a sequence of actions that achieve the goal, such as Breadth-First Search or A*
Reactive Planning — integrating planning with real-time execution to handle changing environments
Hierarchical Planning — breaking down complex tasks into smaller sub-tasks using a hierarchical approach

knowledge.what_it_is: Real-time ray tracing is a rendering technique that simulates the way light behaves in the real world by tracing the path of light as it bounces through the scene. It's a crucial feature for achieving photorealistic graphics in games, and is supported by major game engines like Unreal Engine and Unity.
what_you_will_learn: Ray generation — creating rays from camera position to simulate light paths
Intersection detection — finding where rays intersect with objects in the scene
BxDFs (Bidirectional Scattering Distribution Functions) — describing how materials interact with light
BRDFs (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Functions) — calculating how light is scattered by surfaces
SPPM (Stochastic Progressive Photon Mapping) — accelerating ray tracing using photon mapping
Light transport simulation — simulating the way light interacts with objects and environments
Acceleration structures — optimizing ray tracing performance using data structures like k-d trees or octrees

knowledge.what_it_is: Physically-Based Rendering (PBR) is an advanced rendering technique used to create realistic and detailed game environments, inspired by real-world physics and materials. It's a crucial aspect of modern game development, enabling developers to achieve photorealistic visuals with minimal artistic intervention.
what_you_will_learn: Material Models — understanding the different types of material models used in PBR, such as BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function), GGX (Smith-Maxwell distribution function), and Cook-Torrance
Texture Maps — creating and applying texture maps for normal, metallic, roughness, and ambient occlusion to achieve realistic surface appearance
Lighting Models — implementing advanced lighting models like physically-based diffuse and specular lighting, as well as volumetric lighting effects
BRDFs (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Functions) — understanding the mathematical representation of how light interacts with materials, including Cook-Torrance and GGX models
Microfacet Theory — applying microfacet theory to simulate the behavior of small-scale surface features like scratches, roughness, and anisotropy
Spherical Harmonics (SH) Lighting — using SH lighting to efficiently represent complex lighting environments and achieve realistic indirect lighting effects
PBR Materials in Unity/Unreal Engine — understanding how to implement PBR materials in popular game engines, including material creation, texture baking, and shader setup

knowledge.what_it_is: Microsurface scattering is a technique used to simulate the way light interacts with small-scale surface features, such as tiny bumps, scratches, or pores, on materials like fabric, wood, or skin. It's essential for creating realistic and detailed textures in game development.
what_you_will_learn: Physically-Based Microsurface Scattering (PBMS) — a technique to simulate the way light scatters at small scales using physically-based models
Microfacet Theory — an extension of the Cook-Torrance model for simulating microsurface scattering, considering the distribution and orientation of microscopic features
GGX Distribution — a common method for modeling the distribution of microfacets on surfaces, used in many rendering engines
Smith-Gamma Heuristic — a technique to estimate the amount of diffuse and specular light scattered by microsurfaces, based on the surface roughness
Trowbridge-Reitz Normal Distribution Function (NDF) — a mathematical function describing the distribution of normal vectors at small scales, used in many rendering engines
Spherical Harmonics (SH) — a way to represent and store lighting information for efficient rendering and simulation of microsurface scattering effects

knowledge.what_it_is: DirectX Ray Tracing (DXR) is a rendering technique used in Microsoft's DirectX API to accelerate ray tracing operations on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs. It allows developers to create realistic lighting, reflections, and shadows in real-time.
what_you_will_learn: Ray generation — creating rays from the camera or other sources for intersection testing
Intersection testing — determining which objects intersect with a given ray, using acceleration structures like Bounding Volume Hierarchies (BVH)
Miss handling — handling cases where no object intersects with a ray, including background rendering and ambient occlusion
Ray tracing pipelines — understanding the different stages of the DXR pipeline, including geometry, shading, and lighting
Acceleration structures — optimizing intersection testing using data structures like BVH, k-d trees, or octrees
Material and shader support — integrating custom materials and shaders with DXR's built-in features
Light linking — efficiently rendering complex light scenes using DXR's dynamic light linking feature

knowledge.what_it_is: Translucency and transparency are visual effects used to create realistic glass, water, and other semi-transparent materials in games. They involve manipulating how light interacts with these surfaces, creating a sense of depth and atmosphere.
what_you_will_learn: Alpha blending — combining translucent objects with the background using alpha values
Depth peeling — rendering multiple layers of transparent objects to create accurate occlusion
Screen space ambient occlusion (SSAO) — simulating ambient occlusion for semi-transparent surfaces
Transparency sorting — ordering transparent objects by distance from the camera to prevent artifacts
Layered blending — combining translucent objects with a layered background using additive and subtractive blending modes
Mipmap-based transparency — using mipmapped textures to improve performance when rendering transparent objects
Screen space reflections (SSR) — simulating reflections on semi-transparent surfaces, such as water or glass
Volume rendering — rendering 3D volumes with complex transparency effects, like fog or mist

knowledge.what_it_is: Vulkan Ray Tracing is a feature of the Vulkan graphics API that allows developers to perform ray tracing operations on GPUs, enabling advanced lighting and shading effects in games.
what_you_will_learn: Ray tracing pipelines — creating and managing Vulkan pipeline objects for ray tracing
Acceleration structures — building and using acceleration structures (e.g. BVH, SAH) for efficient ray intersection testing
Ray generation shaders — writing shaders that generate primary rays for the ray tracing process
Intersection shaders — handling intersections between rays and geometry in the scene
Any-hit shaders — controlling how the renderer handles multiple hits from a single ray
Miss shaders — handling cases where no intersection is found (e.g. out-of-bounds, invalid input)
Shader binding tables (SBTs) — managing SBTs for efficient shader execution and memory access
Ray tracing state management — understanding how to manage the ray tracing state in Vulkan

knowledge.what_it_is: The conservation of energy principle states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another. In game development, this concept is crucial for simulating realistic physics and animations.
what_you_will_learn: Energy types — understanding the different forms of energy (kinetic, potential, thermal, etc.) and how they are converted in game environments
Kinematic equations — applying mathematical formulas to calculate velocity, acceleration, and energy transfer between objects
Collision response — simulating energy dissipation during collisions using techniques like impulse-based methods or penalty-based methods
Rigid body dynamics — implementing energy-conserving algorithms for simulating rigid bodies in motion (e.g., Verlet integration)
Soft body simulations — applying energy-based methods to simulate deformable objects and fluids (e.g., SPH, FEM)
Energy-based animation — using conservation of energy principles to drive character animations and movements
Physics engines — integrating game physics engines like PhysX, Havok, or Bullet to utilize built-in energy conservation features

knowledge.what_it_is: OptiX is an advanced, high-performance rendering engine developed by NVIDIA, designed specifically for real-time ray tracing and global illumination in games. It provides a flexible, programmable pipeline that can be customized to achieve optimal performance on various hardware platforms.
what_you_will_learn: Ray Tracing — the fundamental concept of OptiX, enabling accurate simulations of light transport in complex scenes
Acceleration Structures — hierarchical data structures used to accelerate ray tracing operations and reduce memory usage
Programmable Pipelines — customizing the rendering pipeline using NVIDIA's CUDA programming model for optimal performance
Global Illumination — advanced lighting techniques enabled by OptiX, including ambient occlusion, screen-space ambient occlusion, and volumetric lighting
Light Transport — understanding how light interacts with objects in a scene, including diffuse, specular, and glossy reflections
Material Shaders — writing custom shaders to simulate complex material properties, such as anisotropy and subsurface scattering
OptiX API — learning the NVIDIA OptiX API for C++, including functions for creating acceleration structures, launching ray tracing kernels, and accessing rendering results

knowledge.what_it_is: Metallicity refers to the measure of how much an object or material scatters light, which is crucial in game development for realistic lighting and shading. In computer graphics, it's often used to describe the amount of diffuse reflection that occurs when light interacts with a surface.
what_you_will_learn: Physically-Based Metal-Rough (PBR) — understanding how metallicity affects material appearance using physically-based rendering techniques
Metallic Roughness Texture — creating and applying textures that control metallicity and roughness in games, including normal mapping and detail textures
Specular Color vs. Metallic Factor — learning the difference between specular color and metallic factor in PBR materials, and how to use them effectively
Marmoset Toolbag vs. Substance Painter — comparing and contrasting two popular tools for creating and editing PBR materials with metallicity control
Metallic Reflections in Real-Time Ray Tracing (RTRT) — understanding how RTRT handles metallic reflections, including the use of acceleration structures and BVH trees
Dielectric-Metal Transition (DMT) — learning about the DMT model for simulating the transition between dielectric and metal materials, and its applications in game development
